title: Citric acid and bicarbonate toilet cleaning powder
subtitle: A dry fizzing powder with citric acid, bicarbonate of soda, and peppermint essential oil for toilet bowl cleaning
excerpt: Citric acid and bicarbonate of soda react on contact with water, releasing carbon dioxide bubbles that agitate the bowl and loosen limescale and mineral deposits. Peppermint essential oil adds a clean scent. Sprinkle around the bowl, leave for 5 to 10 minutes, then scrub and flush. Yield is 200 g.
sourceNotes: Citric acid and sodium bicarbonate reaction: in dry form the two are stable together; when they contact water the acid donates a proton to the bicarbonate, releasing CO2 and forming sodium citrate. The fizzing agitates the water and the carbonic acid transiently formed helps loosen mineral deposits and limescale. This is the same chemistry as bath bombs and fizzing denture tablets. Peppermint EO in a toilet product: safe; flushed away with the water. Storage note: the dry powder blend absorbs moisture from the air slowly; the airtight container is important. Do not mix with water before use.

recipe.batchNotes: Scale by multiplying all weights. Store in an airtight jar or container.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Ready to use immediately. Keep sealed between uses.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: 6 months in a sealed, airtight container in a dry place. The powder slowly absorbs moisture from the air, which partially activates the fizz and reduces effectiveness. If the powder has clumped, it will still clean but fizz less vigorously.

glossaryTerms.definition: The release of gas bubbles from a liquid, caused here by the reaction between citric acid and bicarbonate of soda in the presence of water. The carbon dioxide bubbles agitate the liquid, helping loosen deposits on the bowl surface.

content.text: Citric acid and bicarbonate of soda are stable as a dry mix; in water they react with

and release carbon dioxide bubbles. The fizzing helps loosen limescale and mineral deposits from hard water. Peppermint essential oil dissolves into the dry mix and releases its scent when the powder hits water. Keep the powder completely dry; moisture triggers a partial reaction and reduces the cleaning effect next time.

items.prepNote: The acidic component; reacts with bicarbonate in water
The alkaline component; reacts with citric acid in water to fizz
Add last and stir in immediately; the oil absorbs into the dry powder

content.text: Weigh the citric acid and bicarbonate of soda into a dry bowl. Stir to combine. Add the peppermint essential oil and stir briskly; the oil will absorb into the dry powder. Transfer immediately to an airtight jar or container and seal. Label with the batch date.

Sprinkle 2 to 3 tablespoons (approximately 20 g) around the inside of the toilet bowl, aiming for the waterline and under the rim. It will fizz on contact with the water. Leave for 5 to 10 minutes to allow the acid to work on limescale deposits. Scrub with a toilet brush and flush. For a heavily scaled bowl, use a larger dose and leave for 20 to 30 minutes before scrubbing.

Keep in an airtight container in a dry place away from the bathroom's humidity. Seal after each use. The powder keeps 6 months; if it clumps, it will still fizz and clean but less vigorously. Discard and make a fresh batch if the powder has fully hardened.
